artifacts.content:
From: laura.castillo@riverbendca.gov
To: javier.martinez@email.com
Subject: Mom, Javi, and this project all colliding
Date: May 20, 2024

Hey Javi,

I’m hiding in a conference room between Riverbend Mixed-Use Village meetings and just needed to brain-dump to someone who gets all the overlapping craziness.

First, Mom: I drove past her Rio Verde senior apartments yesterday and saw the old public notice still taped up about the January 2024 Morrison Creek encampment cleanup behind her building. Hard to believe that’s the same creek segment I’m mapping as “within 2 miles” of the project. It still makes me sick that your ex got that loitering citation down by the culvert when what she really needed was treatment, not tickets.

Then there’s the flood stuff. Every time I look at the American River levee maps for the Village, I flash back to sandbagging behind Riverbend Elementary with you and Daniel during that January 2023 atmospheric river, trying to keep Mateo’s wing from flooding like in 2019 when families lost their uninsured cars. And now Mom tells everyone at Rio Verde that “my daughter is the planner who got us fast-tracked utilities and air filters” after that September 19, 2023 confidential settlement on the mold/HVAC mess in her building. I wish she wouldn’t say that part out loud.

On top of it, Council keeps asking about the emergency zoning text amendment from March 12 that let them use the Village site as overflow evacuation and cooling shelter for residents from Cypress Mobile Home Park after the January flood. She was literally sitting in a cot in the middle of my project site, drinking bad coffee.

Anyway, how are you and Sofía holding up? Did you ever sort out that $6,500 workers’ comp settlement from the U.S. 50/Folsom on‑ramp crash last August 18? Call me tonight if you want to vent.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Mom, Javi, and this project all colliding

artifacts.content:
From: laura.castillo@riverbendca.gov
To: daniel.castillo@email.com
Subject: Car stuff, kids, and this “one less car” dream
Date: April 10, 2024

Dan,

I just biked home along South River Road and had to ride past the exact spot where you got the DUI in November 2019. I still remember sitting in the kitchen that night, talking about how we didn’t want the car to run our lives anymore. Funny and painful that the whole “families can realistically live with one fewer car” line in the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village description basically came from that conversation.

Since your March 2023 DUI on the other side of town and the restricted license that only lets you drive to Riverbend High, it’s gotten very real. Most days I’m on the Route 38 bus or my bike to City Hall and evening hearings, juggling Elena’s clarinet practice and Mateo’s soccer along Morrison Creek. On Tuesdays and Thursdays since January, I’ve been using Lyft so I can drop Elena at John Muir early for her social skills counseling group and still make my 8:00 a.m. coordination meetings.

I keep thinking how different it would be if the pediatric clinic and the little community center we’re planning were already open near East Willow and South River. Elena’s twice‑weekly anxiety sessions could be a short walk instead of the drive to UC Davis Children’s or Kaiser Point West. Same with Mateo’s asthma checkups—no more crossing half the county.

Anyway, I’ll be home a little late—want to swing by Mom’s and drop off groceries. Text me if you need a ride home from Riverbend High; I know the panic attack on U.S. 50 by Folsom in May 2020 still makes freeway driving hard.

Love,
L

metadata.subject: Car stuff, kids, and this “one less car” dream

artifacts.content:
Title: Late-night brain dump – project, kids, Mom
Date: March 28, 2024
Location: Kitchen table, 315 W Cypress

I need to capture how tangled everything feels so I don’t just carry it around in my chest.

Rail + schools: In March 2024 I’ve been double‑checking the Union Pacific Sacramento Subdivision alignment for the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village noise section and realizing it’s the same corridor that runs behind John Muir Middle School and about half a mile from Riverbend Elementary. Elena started 6th grade at John Muir in August 2023, and then in February 2024 a student was clipped by a train near the Folsom Boulevard crossing. I told her about my own near‑miss on that line back in 1999 at CSUS to scare her into staying off the tracks. At the same time, Mateo had that meltdown during recess in March when a long freight went by; his school psychologist asked me to track the loud nighttime trains passing our house on West Cypress for six weeks and compare them to his sleep app data.

Highways + health: Within 2 miles of the site we’ve got SR‑16 and U.S. 50. I can’t separate that from Elena’s school bus sideswipe on westbound 50 near Watt Avenue on September 12, 2021 and the $1,200 CT scan at Mercy Children’s, or Mom’s Chapter 7 call about her $18,000 Riverbend General medical debt that I took pulled over on 50 by Bradshaw on November 10, 2023.

Mom: Her COPD, the Rio Verde mold/HVAC settlement on September 19, 2023, the January 2024 American River flood that sent her to the Village site as an evacuee—it’s all literally the same map I stare at all day.

No wonder I’m exhausted.

metadata.title: Late-night brain dump – project, kids, Mom

artifacts.content:
From: laura.castillo@riverbendca.gov
To: marisol.udavis@gmail.com
Subject: Why this project feels so personal lately
Date: February 16, 2024

Marisol,

I’ve been thinking about your text asking why I seem extra wound‑up about Riverbend Mixed-Use Village, and the honest answer is that it’s sitting on top of half my family’s trauma map.

The site is at East Willow and South River, right across from John Muir. That’s Elena’s walk route now that she started 6th grade in August 2023. I still can’t shake the March 2024 “curb‑jump” crash at that same intersection when a driver hopped the curb and hit a student walking home. Add in the April 22, 2024 near‑miss where a speeding pickup almost hit Elena in the crosswalk, and you get why I pushed Council so hard on May 7 for that School Safety and Traffic Management Overlay around the project.

Then there’s Mateo. His 3rd‑grade class at Riverbend Elementary had that near‑miss with a speeding truck in the crosswalk at East Willow/South River during the November 15, 2023 field trip. I ended up filing a complaint with Riverbend PD’s traffic unit. Same intersection I’m supposed to be “activating” with ground‑floor retail.

Overlay all of that on the January 2023 atmospheric river, when Daniel and I spent three hours sandbagging behind Riverbend Elementary along Morrison Creek after the principal called about possible flooding near Mateo’s wing, remembering the 2019 storm that ruined uninsured cars. Now Morrison Creek and the American River are just bullet points in my “within 2 miles waterways” section.

And Mom—68, COPD, retired MA—lives in a senior complex that keeps popping up in my off‑site utility exhibits.

So yes, I’m a little attached.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Why this project feels so personal lately

artifacts.content:
Title: Big day of hearings + family logistics
Date: March 12, 2024
Location: Riverbend City Hall / around town
Attendees: Laura, Daniel, Elena, Mateo, Mom (Rosa)

All‑day notes:

7:00–7:45 a.m. – Breakfast chaos at 315 W Cypress. Pack lunches, double‑check Elena’s inhaler for school. Quick reminder to Daniel about his Route 38 bus option if he doesn’t feel up to driving to Riverbend High; his panic attack near the U.S. 50/Folsom interchange back on May 6, 2020 still lingers.

7:45–8:15 a.m. – Drive kids: drop Mateo at Riverbend Elementary, then Elena at John Muir. Avoid East Willow/South River if possible because of my own minor collision there in October 2022 and the way my chest tightens now when I see that intersection.

8:30–11:30 a.m. – Prep for Council. Review slides on General Plan Amendment, Rezone to PUD, Use Permits, and the Mitigated Negative Declaration. Quick side huddle with staff about the emergency zoning text amendment Council is set to adopt tonight so they can keep using the Mixed-Use Village site as overflow evacuation and cooling shelter for folks from Cypress Mobile Home Park displaced by the January American River flood (including Mom).

12:00–12:30 p.m. – Walk loop around downtown instead of eating at desk. Call Javier about his Safe Parking overlay idea; remind him I haven’t forgotten those three weeks in January when he and Sofía were sleeping in his HVAC van near Riverbend Elementary after his hours got cut.

5:30–9:00 p.m. – City Council meeting. Agenda includes: emergency zoning text amendment (shelter use), School Safety and Traffic Management Overlay initiation near John Muir, and direction on floodplain/air quality mitigation.

9:30 p.m. – Swing by Mom’s senior apartment to drop off groceries and check on her breathing.

metadata.title: Big day of hearings + family logistics

artifacts.content:
From: laura.castillo@riverbendca.gov
To: mom.rosa@email.com
Subject: Plan for your appointments & the new clinic
Date: April 5, 2025

Mami,

I’m glad you’re back home from Mercy General after the ER visit on April 3 for your blood sugar. You scared us. I talked to Dr. Nguyen at Riverbend Community Health yesterday about that twice‑monthly diabetes management group she’s planning to run in the little neighborhood clinic space at the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village once it opens. She said you’d be a perfect candidate, especially since it’s so close to Rio Verde.

I know you hate driving now, especially after your minor collision turning left out of the senior complex onto South River Road back in October 2023. Remember, Javier and I will handle rides. Once the clinic opens, you could just take the shuttle instead of worrying about that intersection.

You asked me why I keep fighting for the community center and clinic. Part of it is you, part of it is the kids. Elena’s anxiety got so much worse after the June 2022 near‑drowning at the American River by Watt Avenue and then the October 9, 2022 asthma attack at John Muir that ended with her being airlifted to the Riverbend Medical Center rooftop helipad. Right now we still drive all the way to UC Davis Children’s and Kaiser Point West for her weekly sessions, but Riverbend Health Partners is talking about putting a low‑cost pediatric behavioral health clinic in the ground‑floor commercial space. If that happens, her Fall 2026 therapy could be a short walk instead of a freeway trip.

We’ll get through this, one appointment at a time. I’ll pick you up Tuesday at 3:00 for the follow‑up at Mercy Riverbend Clinic.

Te quiero,
Laura

metadata.subject: Plan for your appointments & the new clinic

artifacts.content:
Title: Work/ethics tangle – private reflections
Date: November 20, 2023
Location: OneNote – personal

I need a place to be honest about the stuff that never makes it into staff reports.

Landlords: I’m still rattled by that November 2022 closed‑door meeting at the Riverbend Chamber of Commerce where Mark Delaney from the landlord group basically told me that if I didn’t “soften” the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village EIR discussion of displacement risk and rent hikes in the older West Cypress apartments (including Mom’s building), they’d oppose my reappointment to the Housing Element Technical Advisory Committee. It felt like a threat, and it’s been in the back of my mind every time I write about population/housing balance and environmental justice.

Schools: Within 2 miles of the site we’ve got Riverbend Elementary, John Muir, and Riverbend High. Those same three schools are where so much of our family drama lives—Mateo’s April 2023 racist bullying complaint with Riverbend Unified, Elena’s December 2022 CPS report after she saw a classmate being hit by his stepfather in the Riverbend High parking lot, Daniel’s March 2024 parent emails about vaping behind the portables.

Rail/highway: While I was drafting the “within 2 miles railways” section in March 2024, I realized I’d been timing late‑night freight horns during Elena’s Thursday 6:30–7:30 p.m. anxiety sessions at Riverbend Child & Family Counseling since October 2023 to see if they lined up with Mom’s worst insomnia nights at the F Street senior complex. Same with the “within 2 miles state highways” box—every time I type U.S. 50 and SR‑16, I think about my own March 3, 2022 DUI on eastbound 50 near Bradshaw.

Hard to be the “objective planner” when the map is basically a family diary.

From: Laura Marisol Castillo <laura.castillo@riverbendca.gov>
To: Javier Martinez <j.martinez@example.com>
Date: May 6, 2025 9:14 PM
Subject: Mom, the project name, and whether this feels okay

Hey Javi,

I wanted to give you a heads-up about something before it becomes fully public. For the CEQA documents on the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village project area, I’ve been using a working title that folds in the supportive housing component. Right now the draft reads:

“Riverbend Mixed-Use Village and Supportive Housing for Survivors of Domestic Violence – Rosa Martinez Campus (CEQA Initial Study completed August 2025).”

On paper it makes sense: the City of Riverbend has been trying to signal that we’re serious about centering survivors, and staff really responded when I floated the idea of naming the campus after Mom. But I keep tripping over whether it’s fair to her to have “survivors of domestic violence” and “Rosa Martinez Campus” linked so explicitly in a public record.

I haven’t talked to her directly about it yet—just hinted that there might be some kind of dedication if the project is approved. Before I go any further, I need your honest read. Do you think she’d feel honored, or exposed? You remember how private she was about everything that happened when we were kids.

I’m also wrestling with my own role in this as both her daughter and the Senior Planner/Project Manager. It’s hard to separate the personal from the professional when the project title literally carries our mom’s name.

Can we talk this weekend, maybe after Sofía’s soccer practice?

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Mom, the project name, and whether this feels okay

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Marisol Castillo <laura.castillo@riverbendca.gov>
To: City Attorney’s Office <cityattorney@riverbendca.gov>
Cc: Planning Director <director.planning@riverbendca.gov>
Date: June 18, 2025 3:42 PM
Subject: Legal review of CEQA project titles – reentry & DV components

Hi all,

As we finalize CEQA materials for the City of Riverbend’s Riverbend Mixed-Use Village project area, I need guidance on two sensitive project titles I’ve been using in draft.

1) Reentry housing component: My current working title is “Riverbend Mixed-Use Village and Court-Mandated Reentry Housing for Former Offenders from Sacramento County Superior Court Case Dockets 18-CR-42791 and 19-CR-55302.” Conceptually, this mirrors how the Sacramento County Superior Court describes the referral program, but I’m increasingly uncomfortable that we may be “branding” participants by their case numbers in a permanent public document. From a legal/privacy standpoint, is it appropriate to reference “Former Offenders from Sacramento County Superior Court Case Dockets 18-CR-42791 and 19-CR-55302” directly in the CEQA project title, or should we generalize (e.g., “court-mandated reentry housing program”)?

2) Supportive housing for survivors: For the domestic violence supportive housing, my draft title is “Riverbend Mixed-Use Village and Supportive Housing for Survivors of Domestic Violence – Rosa Martinez Campus (CEQA Initial Study completed August 2025).” Here the issue is less about the use of “survivors” and more about naming the campus after my mother, Rosa Martinez, whose survivorship is not widely known.

As Project Manager on both components, I want to balance transparency, trauma-informed practice, and liability. Could you provide written direction on acceptable naming conventions before we lock the August 2025 Initial Study and related notices?

Best,

Laura Marisol Castillo
Senior Planner / Project Manager
City of Riverbend Community Development Department

metadata.subject: Legal review of CEQA project titles – reentry & DV components
